segments.s562-g1: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause; بر + گاه forms a prepositional phrase 'to/onto the court/throne' (بر governs گاه), رفت is the past of رفتن 'went', and اورمزد بزرگ is the proper name with an epithet 'the great Ormazd' (noun phrase as the subject of رفت). Word order is poetic but function is: 'When (چو) Ormazd the Great went to the court.'
segments.s562-g2: ز marks source or cause ('from' / 'out of'), نخچیر is the noun 'spoil/prey', کوتاه شد is a two-word verbal phrase meaning 'was shortened' (شد is past of شدن ‹become/‑ed›), and چنگ گرگ is a genitive noun phrase 'the wolf's claw' (چنگ modified by گرگ). The verb phrase appears before the possessed noun for poetic order: 'from the spoil the wolf's claw was shortened.'
segments.s562-g3: جهان را is the definite direct object with the object-marker را; همی is the archaic continuous/adverbial particle often used with past tense to indicate ongoing state; داشت here functions as a past-tense verb 'kept/maintained' (literally 'was having/holding'), and با ایمنی is an instrumental/advocative phrase 'with safety' describing the manner. Together: 'he kept the world with safety.'
segments.s562-g4: نهان is an adjectival/adverbial 'hidden', گشت is the past of گشتن used here as 'became/was turned' (resultative), and کردار آهرمنی is a noun phrase 'the deed/action of Ahriman' (آهرمنی as genitive/attributive). The clause is therefore 'Ahriman's deed became hidden.' Note the verb precedes the noun phrase for poetic inversion.
segments.s562-g5: نخست is an adverb 'first'; آفرین here is a noun 'praise/acclamation' that functions as the object of کرد (a light verb 'did/made'), so آفرین کرد = 'gave praise' / 'praised'; بر marks the indirect object 'to/unto' and کردگار is 'the Creator/God' receiving the praise. The construction is a common Persian light-verb phrase 'آفرین کردن بر X.'
segments.s562-g6: توانا و دانا و پروردگار is a series of epithets joined by و modifying کردگار (elliptically repeating 'the Creator, the Powerful, the Wise, and the Lord'). Each adjective follows the head noun in apposition, a poetic epithet-list.
segments.s562-g7: شب و روز و گردان سپهر is a coordinated noun phrase 'night and day and the revolving sky/heavens' (گردان is an adjective 'revolving' modifying سپهر), and آفرید is the past tense verb 'created.' The typical Persian order places the list of objects before the verb: 'he created night and day and the revolving heavens.'
segments.s562-g8: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when/as' and is followed by a coordinated list of names (بهرام, کیوان, مهر) which are objects of the verb آفرید that closes the clause; thus 'when he created Bahram and Kayvān and Mehr.' The proper names here refer to celestial/astrological figures and are coordinated with و.
segments.s562-g9: ازویست is a contracted poetic form of از وی است (literally “it is from him/it”), placed at the start to attribute the following qualities to that person. The clause then lists coordinated nouns (پیروزی و فرهی) linked by و; فرهی is an adjective/noun meaning ‘nobility/glory’ used as a nominal quality. Word order (predicate before items) is typical of Persian poetic inversion.
segments.s562-g10: This continues the coordinated list of qualities attributed by ازویست; دل و داد و دیهیم are nouns joined by و, followed by شاهنشهی which scopes as the final specification (‘and the imperial rule’). داد functions as the abstract noun ‘justice’. The sequence is paratactic (nouns piled together) rather than governed by an explicit verb.
segments.s562-g11: همیشه (always) is an adverbial preface; دل ما is the subject (‘our heart’). پر از داد is a predicate phrase meaning ‘full of justice’ (پر از = full of), and باد is the optative particle (‘may’) forming a wish—so the whole clause reads as a blessing: “May our heart always be full of justice.”
segments.s562-g12: دل زیردستان is a genitive-like construction meaning ‘the hearts of those beneath (us)’; به ما shad باد uses به + pronoun (‘toward/for us’) plus شاد (happy) and the optative باد again, yielding ‘may the hearts of our subjects be glad for us’. Word order places the possessed noun before the clause of felicitation.
segments.s562-g13: ستایش نیابد is a negative present (literary) construction meaning ‘praise does not come / is not obtained’; نیابد is the verb ‘to obtain/attain’ in this form. سر سفله مرد is a noun phrase (‘the head/being of a base man’ i.e. a low-born or base man), so the clause means that such a man cannot attain praise.
segments.s562-g14: بر سفلگان is a prepositional phrase ‘against the lowly’; تا توانی is a concessive/time-like phrase here meaning ‘as far as you can’ or ‘to the extent of your ability’; مگرد is a negative imperative (‘do not turn/become’ in older usage) commanding restraint. Together: ‘Do not turn against the lowly as much as you are able.’
segments.s562-g15: همان نیز با مرد بدخواه رای literally: ‘do likewise with a man who is ill-wishing in counsel/opinion.’ همان نیز (‘the same also’) signals that the speaker advises the same treatment here; با introduces the accompaniment/target (with the ill-wishing man), and رای is ‘opinion/counsel’ describing his attitude.
segments.s562-g16: اگر پندگیری = ‘if you take advice’ (pended as a condition). به نیکی گرای is an imperative/instruction meaning ‘turn toward goodness’ (به نیکی = toward goodness); گرای is an older/poetic verbal form meaning ‘turn/incline’ here. The whole conditional: ‘If you accept (his) counsel, turn toward goodness.’
segments.s562-g73: برفتند is a past-plural verb 'they went/departed'. گردان is a noun 'troop/company' placed after the verb (verb-first order is common in classical Persian poetry). زرین is an adjective 'golden' modifying کمر; کمر here means 'girdle/belt' (a metonymic epithet 'gold-belted'). Together they read 'the troop with golden belts went off.'
segments.s562-g74: بیاویختند is a past-plural transitive verb 'they hung/suspended'. تاج زر is the direct object 'a gold crown'. از introduces a source/point of attachment ('from'), so از برش is an adverbial 'from his/its side/edge' (برش functions as the source noun). The phrase order places the source before the object for poetic rhythm.
segments.s562-g75: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'. آن خرد را: آن 'that', خرد here means 'the little one/young child' (adjectival/small sense) and را is the definite object marker. سیر دادند is a light-verb construction 'gave (to) satiety' = 'fed to fullness'; شیر is the object 'milk'. Together: 'When they had fed that little one full of milk...'.
segments.s562-g76: نوشتند is past-plural 'they wrote/inscribed' (the specific direct object is implied rather than stated). پس is an adverb 'then/after that' linking the action temporally. در میان حریر is a prepositional phrase 'in/among silk' (در + میان = 'in the midst of') specifying the medium/material on which they wrote (i.e., on a piece of silk).
segments.s562-g77: چهل روزه را is a noun phrase 'the forty‑day‑old' marked by را as the definite object. (The printed lemma 'روزه' in the word list does not supply an exact sense for 'days‑old' here, so the listed senses do not match the poetic age adjectival use.) زیر آن تاج زر is a prepositional phrase 'under that gold crown' describing where the child was placed; note the prepositional phrase precedes the verb for word‑order variety.
segments.s562-g78: نهادند is the past-plural verb 'they placed/set' (the verbal sense of نهاد). بر تخت is 'upon the throne' (بر = on/upon, تخت = throne). فرخ پدر is a genitive/attributive sequence 'the fortunate/happy father' qualifying whose throne it is; Persian places the possessor/qualifier after the possessed noun in such constructions. The whole segment completes the action: 'they placed (him) upon the fortunate father's throne.'
segments.s562-g17: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/regarding'; here it marks the topic ('with regard to the giving'). بخشش is the noun 'giving/grant' (modified by the following relative clause). هرانکس که جوید سپاس is a relative clause 'whoever/anyone who seeks thanks' where که introduces the clause and جوید is a classical present-tense verb 'seeks' taking سپاس as its object; word order places the relative clause after the noun it qualifies.
segments.s562-g18: نخواندش is the negative present (classical) form of خواندن with the enclitic object -ش 'him', literally 'will not call/name him'. Here خواندن is used in the sense 'to call/name (someone as something)'. بخشنده یزدان‌شناس is the subject in apposition ('the generous, God-knowing one'); in older/poetic Persian the verb often comes before its subject, so the verb precedes this nominal subject.
segments.s562-g19: ستاننده‌گر is an agentive compound 'the one who exacts/collects' (a doer noun). ناسپاست combines ناسپاس ('ungrateful') with the copula -است in one word (common in classical/poetic Persian) meaning 'is ungrateful'. نیز is the adverb 'also/likewise' following the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s562-g20: This clause should be read as 'no one deems him fit for anything' — سزد‌گر functions as 'one who deems fit' (agentive/verb-like), and ندارد کس ... is a negative existential construction ('there is no one...'). او را is the object/topic marker attached to 'him', and به چیز means 'for anything' (چیز = 'thing', used here as 'anything'). The verb ندارد combined with کس yields 'there is no one who...'.
segments.s562-g21: هراسان is a predicative adjective 'frightened'; بود is the past copula 'was/were' placed after the adjective (typical older/poetic word order). مردم سخت‌کار is the subject 'the hardworking people' (مردم modified by سخت‌کار).
segments.s562-g22: که introduces a subordinate clause of content 'that...'. او را is the object/topic 'him' (را marking definite object/emphasis). نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive form of بودن used here after که to express 'there be/should be not' (i.e., 'there would be no'). کسی دوستدار literally 'anyone fond of' — together the phrase means 'that there be no one fond of him' (i.e., 'that no one will love/like him'). Note the classical inversion and the use of نباشد to express fear/possibility following هراسان بود.
segments.s562-g23: وگر is the conditional 'if' (or 'but if'). سستی 'laxity/sloth' is the subject of آرد, the classical present-tense verb 'brings' (آرد = 'brings/causes to come'). به کار اندرون is a prepositional/directional phrase 'into the inner work' (i.e., into household/inner affairs), with به indicating movement/tendency 'into' and اندرون 'inner/within'.
segments.s562-g24: نخواند is again the negative present of خواندن; here it uses the sense 'to call/summon' and takes the enclitic object ورا 'to him'. رای‌زن رهنمون are two nouns in apposition 'counselor, guide' serving as the subject (placed after the verb by poetic word order). The clause thus means 'no counselor/guide will call him' or 'he will not be summoned by counselor or guide.'
segments.s562-g33: وگر is the conditional particle 'if'. بازگیرند is a 3rd-person-plural present/subjunctive-like form meaning 'they take back' or 'they seize back' (classical/political nuance 'reclaim, recover'). ازو is the prepositional clitic 'from him'. خواسته here is a noun ('that which was asked/requested' or 'the demanded thing') rather than a simple adjective; the whole clause sets up the condition whose result follows in the next segment.
segments.s562-g34: شود is the auxiliary/predicative 'be / will be' (classical present/future sense). جان و مغز و دلش is a coordinated list 'his life and brain and heart' — the possessive ش is attached to the final noun but applies to the whole series. کاسته is a past-participle/adjectival form meaning 'diminished' or 'lessened' and functions as the predicate described by شود ('will be diminished').
segments.s562-g35: به here marks direction or the state 'into / by', so به بی چیزی و بدخویی introduces the forces affecting him: 'poverty and ill-temper'. یازد is a classical verb meaning 'burst forth / assail / break out' (here 'fell upon / assailed'), and اوی is the clitic 'him/he' referring back to the person described.
segments.s562-g36: ندارد is the negative present 'does not have' (3rd person). خرد is the noun 'wisdom' and is the thing he lacks. گردن افرازد اوی functions as a relative/result clause — 'that would raise his neck/head' — where افرازد is a classical verbal form 'would raise/exalt' and اوی again marks 'his/he'. The whole phrase means he lacks the very wisdom that would make him hold his head high.
segments.s562-g37: The repeated نه ... و نه ... construction is simple negation 'neither ... nor ...'. The nouns are listed as lacking qualities: چیز 'thing/possessions', دانش 'learning/knowledge', رای 'counsel/opinion/plan', هنر 'art/skill' — a poetic catalogue of what he does not possess.
segments.s562-g38: Continuation of the negative catalogue: نه دین 'nor religion/faith' and نه خشنودی دادگر 'nor the approval/favor of the just'. In classical Persian the genitive/possessive relation (approval of the just) can appear without an overt ezafe; دادگر here is the agent/qualifier 'the just (one / judge / righteous ruler)'.
segments.s562-g39: شما را uses را in a dative/benefactive sense 'for you' (a fixed formula for blessings). شب و روز 'night and day' is the time phrase, فرخنده 'auspicious/fortunate', and باد is the optative particle 'may it be' — a conventional formula to wish someone well.
segments.s562-g40: Another optative/wish formula aimed at the enemy: بداندیش را 'to the ill‑intentioned one' (dative), جان 'life/soul', پراگنده 'scattered/destroyed', and باد again the optative 'may it be' — together: 'may the malicious man's life be scattered'.
segments.s562-g65: به سر برش تاجی برآویختند = a single clause meaning 'they hung a crown on his head.' به سر is a prepositional phrase 'onto/on the head of...', where برش is a poetic contracted phrase: بر + -ش (third-person enclitic) = 'on him' / 'his head'. تاجی is an indefinite object 'a crown' placed before the verb. برآویختند is past, 3rd person plural (بر‑آویخت + اند) meaning 'they hung/fastened upon'.
segments.s562-g66: بران تاج زر و درم ریختند = 'upon that (crown) they poured/scattered gold and dirhams.' بران ('upon that') refers back to the crown just placed; تاج زر و درم is a coordinated noun phrase ('gold and dirhams') that is the object of ریختند. ریختند is past 3rd pl. of ریختن used here as 'to pour/scatter/bestow'.
segments.s562-g67: چهل روز بگذشت بر خوب‌چهر = 'Forty days passed for/over the fair‑faced (one).' چهل روز is a duration (forty days) and بگذشت is the past 3rd singular 'passed.' بر + خوب‌چهر ('on/for the fair‑faced') marks the person affected by that time span — a common poetic use meaning 'as far as the fair‑faced one is concerned.'
segments.s562-g68: یکی کودک آمد چو تابنده مهر = 'a certain child came, like the shining Sun.' یکی is an indefinite article-like 'a certain' (introducing someone), کودک is the subject 'child,' آمد is simple past 'came/appeared.' چو introduces a simile 'like/as,' and تابنده (a participle used adjectivally) modifies مهر; here مهر is used poetically as the radiant sun/solar deity, so the whole simile describes the child's brightness.
segments.s562-g69: ورا موبدش نام شاپور کرد = 'to him his mobed gave the name Shapur' (i.e. 'his priest named him Shapur'). ورا is an object marker 'to him' (or 'him'); موبدش is موبد (mobed, Zoroastrian priest) with the possessive enclitic -ش 'his' = 'his mobed'; نام + شاپور is the name assigned; کرد is the past 3rd singular of کردن used to mean 'made/gave (a name)' or 'called.'
segments.s562-g70: بران شادمانی یکی سور کرد = 'upon that joy they held a feast.' بران ('on/for that') again points back to the preceding event; شادمانی is 'joy/rejoicing.' یکی سور کرد is an idiomatic, indefinite phrasing meaning 'they made a feast' (یکی here introduces the event as 'a certain' or simply 'a' feast) and کرد is 'did/made' (past).
segments.s562-g71: تو گفتی همی فره ایزدیست = 'You said / you declared: indeed the farr is divine.' تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun 'you,' گفتی here is the past 2nd‑person verb 'you said' (note: the provided dictionary senses listed 'گفتی' as 'conversation' but in context it functions as the verb 'you said'). همی is an emphatic/continuous particle ('indeed/just' or marking the quoted statement), and فره ایزدیست is a predicate nominal: فره 'farr' (divine royal glory) + ایزدیست 'is divine' (ایزدی + است).
segments.s562-g72: برو سایه رایت بخردیست = an imperative-like sequence: برو ('go' / 'be gone') followed by a statement 'سایه رایت بخردیست' — 'the shadow/shelter of the banner is wise/prudent.' سایه رایت literally 'the shadow of the standard/banner' used figuratively as 'the protection/shelter of the standard'; بخردیست (ب‑خرد‑ی‑ست) means 'it is wise' or 'it is prudent.' The two parts together function as a dismissal plus a comment about the safety/wise protection under the banner.
segments.s562-g25: گر is the conditional particle 'if' at the clause head. از + noun (از کاهلان) marks 'from/among the lazy' as the source for the choice; کاهلان is the plural 'lazy (people)'. یار is the noun 'companion' and functions as the thing you 'seek' or 'want' (it is the object of خواهی). خواهی is second-person singular present (you want/seek). به کار is a prepositional phrase 'for work/use' that does not finish its predicate here but links to the following negative verb (نباشی) to yield 'not useful for work'.
segments.s562-g26: نباشی is the negative second-person present form of 'to be' and here functions as the predicate for the previous complement 'به کار' — together meaning 'you will not be useful/fit for work'. The coordinated nouns جهانجوی و مردم‌شمار are predicate/character descriptions joined by و; they list the kinds of person you will not be (a world-seeker/ambitious person and a 'people-counter' or one who weighs/keeps account of people).
segments.s562-g27: نگر is an imperative 'look!/consider' addressing the listener; خویشتن را is the emphatic object 'yourself' (را marks definiteness/emphasis). نداری is a noun/abstract notion of 'lack' or 'poverty' here ('you have a lack' / 'you are without'), and بزرگ qualifies that lack or the expected greatness — together the clause conveys 'consider yourself: you lack greatness' (word order is imperative + object, then comment about the state).
segments.s562-g28: وگر is 'but/if' introducing a conditional clause; گاه 'occasion/occasionally' sets the temporal frame ('if at some time' / 'if ever'). یابی is the second-person form 'you find/attain' (here 'should you attain' / 'if you happen to get'). نگردی is the negative form of گشتن 'to become' — 'you will not become' — and سترگ (literally 'sight/eye' here) functions idiomatically as 'something to be seen/admired' or 'a thing of grandeur' so the clause means 'and if you ever attain it, you will not become (someone) impressive/visible.'
segments.s562-g29: چو is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. بدخو 'ill-tempered' is a predicate adjective describing مرد 'man' and شود is the present third-person of 'to become' (becomes). درویش 'the poor/dervish' is the object of scorn and خوار is a predicate/adjective meaning 'despised/lowly' — the clause reads 'when a man becomes ill-tempered, (the) poor (one) is despised' or 'the man despises the poor.'
segments.s562-g30: همی is an adverb meaning 'always/still' (a habitual marker). بیند is the third-person singular present of 'to see' (here 'he sees/regards'). آن refers back to the preceding fact/situation. از with بد روزگار links cause/instrument: 'because of / from bad fortune' (i.e. he views it as due to ill luck). روزگار is used as 'fortune/fate'. The whole clause states that he habitually perceives that scorn as the result of bad times/fortune.
segments.s562-g31: همه‌ساله is an adverbial 'yearly' or 'year after year'. بیکار و نالان are coordinated adjectives 'idle and lamenting' describing the subject (implied). ز is the poetic form of 'from/because of' and bخت 'fortune/fate' gives the reason: 'idle and lamenting because of fate.' The phrase sets a habitual/state description tied to misfortune.
segments.s562-g32: نه ... و نه ... نه introduces a negative series 'neither ... nor ... nor'. رای here means 'counsel/plan' (lack of influence or counsel), دانش is 'knowledge/learning', and زیبای تخت is 'the beauty/splendour of the throne' (زیبای qualifying تخت). The fragment lists what is lacking: neither good counsel, nor learning, nor the splendour/benefits of rulership.
segments.s562-g49: چنین بود = 'it was thus' (چنین 'thus/so' + بود past 'was'). تا is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'as long as/while'. The second بود is the past copula agreeing with the following subject phrase چرخ روان (verb before subject, a common poetic order); روان modifies چرخ ('the wheel that runs/moves' = wheel of fate).
segments.s562-g50: توانا به هر کار = 'capable/strong in every task' (توانا adjective). به introduces the domain/area ('in/at' every کار 'deed/task'); هر is distributive 'every'. و ما ناتوان links contrastively: و 'and' + ما 'we' + ناتوان 'powerless/incapable'. The whole clause is in past narrative, following the temporal clause before it.
segments.s562-g51: چهل روز = duration 'for forty days' (numeric phrase before the verb). سوکش (سوگ + ـش) is 'his mourning/they mourned him' as the object/theme. همی is an archaic/poetic continuous marker often used with past verbs to show an ongoing action. داشتند is past plural 'they held/kept' (here 'kept mourning').
segments.s562-g52: سر گاه = 'at times / on occasion' (سر + گاه functioning adverbially). او is the pronominal object 'him'. خوار بگذاشتند is an idiomatic verbal phrase: بگذاشتند (they put/left/made) with predicate خوار 'despised' → 'they put him into contempt / treated him as despised'. Word order places the time-expression first, then the object, then the predicative verb.
segments.s562-g53: به چندین زمان = 'for several times/periods' (به marking duration/extent). تخت بیکار بود = 'the throne was idle/vacant' (تخت 'throne' + بیکار 'idle/vacant' + بود past copula). Word order is prepositional duration phrase, then subject + predicate + copula.
segments.s562-g54: سر مهتران = 'the heads of the chiefs' (سر 'head' as possessed noun + مهتران 'chiefs/elders'). پر ز تیمار بود = 'were full of care/worry' (پر 'full (of)' + ز 'of/because of' + تیمار 'care' + بود past copula). The predicate follows the possessed noun in standard Persian noun-adjective order.
segments.s562-g55: نگه کرد = 'kept watch' (نگه 'watch/gaze' + کرد auxiliary 'did/kept'). موبد is the subject ('the mobed/priest'). شبستان شاه is a genitive noun phrase 'the king's bedchamber/prayer-hall' giving the location. The verb phrase is past narrative; subject follows the verb here in poetic inversion.
segments.s562-g56: یکی = 'a certain / one'. لاله رخ is a compound epithet 'tulip-faced' (لاله 'flower/beauty' modifying رخ 'face'). دید is past 'he saw' (subject carried over from previous clause). تابان 'shining' is a postpositive adjective, and چو ماه is a simile 'like the moon'. The whole phrase describes the person the mobed saw.
segments.s562-g57: سر مژه is a compound meaning the 'tip/head of the eyelash' (سر used as 'top/tip'); چون is the comparative 'like/as'; خنجر کابلی is a noun + adjectival/attributive phrase 'Kabul dagger' (adjective after the noun in classical Persian). The whole segment is a simile comparing the eyelash-tip to a dagger.
segments.s562-g58: دو زلفش = 'her two locks' with دو (numeral) before the noun and the enclitic ش marking possession ('her'). چو here again introduces a simile; پیچان is a participial/adjectival form 'twisting/curling'; خط مغولی is an image 'line/stroke like Mongol (script)', i.e. the curls resemble Mongolian strokes. Word order is typical poetic Persian (adjective/participle after the noun or in attributive position).
segments.s562-g59: مسلسل here functions adverbially as 'continuously / in succession'; یک اندر دگر is a classical idiom 'one into another' (یک = one, اندر = in/into, دگر = other/another); بافته is the past participle 'woven'. Together they describe hair woven one into the next in a continuous way.
segments.s562-g60: گره بر زده is a participial phrase meaning 'a knot having been tied / a knot tied upon (it)' where بر + زده indicates 'tied on/placed on'; سرش is 'her head' (سر + enclitic ش = her); برتافته is a past participle/adjectival 'raised/exalted' describing the state of the head. The clause therefore says a knot was tied and her head was held high.
segments.s562-g61: پری چهره is a compound adjective+noun 'fairy‑faced' (a beautiful person); را is the classical definite/object marker applied here to mark بچه ('the child') as the topical/definite object; بچه اندر نهان = 'a child hidden inside' (اندر = in/within, نهان = hidden). The verb is omitted here and supplied by the following clause—this is a compact, classical word order.
segments.s562-g62: ازان = 'thereupon / because of that' links back to the previous clause; خوب‌رخ is 'fair‑faced / beautiful-faced' (the source of joy); شادمان شد is a two-word verbal phrase 'became joyful' (شادمان = joyful, شد = became); جهان 'the world' appears at the end as the subject for emphasis (typical classical word order).
segments.s562-g63: چهل روزه شد is a classical expression meaning 'it became forty days' (i.e. forty days had passed). The token رود in this line is an archaic/poetic element (or possibly a variant form) not well represented in the provided sense list; here it functions as a connective/modal poetic particle indicating the passage/progression of time. و می‌خواستند is the past imperfect 'and they wished/desired'. (Because the supplied sense lists do not include 'day/days' or this archaic use of رود, those items are marked with -1 in senses.)
segments.s562-g64: یکی here is an indefinite/indeterminate 'a certain' or 'one (a)' used before the noun; تخت شاهی is a genitive/compound 'royal throne' (تخت = throne, شاهی = royal/kingship); بیاراستند is the past plural 'they adorned / they prepared' (بیاراستن is a classical compound verb meaning to decorate/prepare). The verb completes the thought 'they wished to prepare a royal throne.'
segments.s562-g41: This is a short clause with an adverb/particle (برو) up front, then the subject مهتران (the elders/leaders) and the object آفرین (an acclamation), finishing with the past-tense verb ساختند 'they made/composed'. The compound آفرین ساختند is a common poetic idiom meaning 'they cried out praise' or 'they praised'.
segments.s562-g42: خود here is a reflexive/emphatic pronoun ('themselves'). از introduces the cause/reason ('on account of' / 'because of') + سوک ('mourning'); شاهان is the genitive ('of the kings'). بپرداختند is past plural of پرداختن and here means 'they attended to / performed / took part in (a rite)', so the whole clause says they themselves engaged in the kings' mourning.
segments.s562-g43: چو نه سال بگذشت is a temporal clause: چو = 'when', نه سال = 'nine years', بگذشت = past verb 'passed' (3rd sing. perf./aorist). بر سر سپهر is a prepositional phrase 'upon the head/top of the sky' (poetic = 'over the heavens'), placed after the time clause for emphasis.
segments.s562-g44: This is a verbless / small-clause style typical in poetry: گل (subject) + زرد شد (predicate: became yellow/pale). آن is a demonstrative ('that' / 'its' in context), followed by a simile introduced by چو ('like/as') and گلنار چهر ('pomegranate-faced' or 'with a face like a pomegranate'). So literally 'the flower became yellow; that is, like a pomegranate-faced one.'
segments.s562-g45: غمی شد is an impersonal/existential phrasing: غم (a grief) + شد ('occurred' / 'came about'). ز (poetic form of از) marks source/cause 'from' or 'because of' مرگ (the death) of آن سر تاجور — 'that head/leader, the merchant' (سر here = 'head' / 'person' referring to the merchant).
segments.s562-g46: بمرد is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'he died' (the b‑ prefix appears in older narrative forms). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and/then'. به شاهی literally 'to/for kingship' (به marks the role/position), نبودش = 'there was not for him' (negated verb with enclitic -ش = 'his/for him'), پسر = 'a son' — together: 'he died and he had no son to the kingship' i.e. no heir.
segments.s562-g47: This is a noun phrase with modifiers: چنان 'such/so' modifies نامور مرد 'a renowned man', and شیرین‌سخن ('sweet‑tongued') is a compound adjective following the noun (common in Persian epithets). The whole phrase describes someone notable in character.
segments.s562-g48: به نوی بشد is a verbal phrase: به نوی 'into newness / newly' (to a new state) + بشد, an older/poetic past/preterite form of شدن ('it became' / 'he/it came to be'). زین سرای کهن literally 'from/for this old house' (زین = ز + این, 'from/of this') — together implying that the old house became renewed or that he appeared newly in this ancient house.

sentences.s562-s1: When the great Ormazd went to the court, the wolf's claw was shortened from the spoil.
sentences.s562-s2: He kept the world in safety, and Ahriman's deed became hidden.
sentences.s562-s3: First he offered praise to the Creator, the Powerful, the Wise, and the Nourisher.
sentences.s562-s4: He created night and day and the revolving heavens; when he created Bahram, Kayvān, and Mehr, ...
sentences.s562-s5: From him come victory and noble glory, heart and justice, and the imperial rule.
sentences.s562-s6: May our heart always be full of justice; may the hearts of our subjects be glad for us.
sentences.s562-s7: A base (low-born) man does not obtain praise; do not turn against the lowly as far as you can.
sentences.s562-s8: Do the same with a man who wishes you ill; if you take his advice, incline toward goodness.
sentences.s562-s37: The gold-belted troop marched off, and they hung a gold crown from his side.
sentences.s562-s38: When they had filled that little one with milk, they then wrote (it) on silk.
sentences.s562-s39: They placed the forty‑day‑old beneath that gold crown on his father's fortunate throne.
sentences.s562-s9: As for the giving of anyone who seeks thanks, the generous, God-knowing will not call him grateful.
sentences.s562-s10: He who exacts is also ungrateful; likewise, no one will deem him deserving of anything.
sentences.s562-s11: The hardworking people were frightened, for they feared that no one would be fond of him.
sentences.s562-s12: But if sloth creeps into his household work, no counselor or guide will call him.
sentences.s562-s17: And if they take back from him what was demanded, his life, brain, and heart will be diminished.
sentences.s562-s18: Poverty and ill temper assailed him; he lacked the wisdom that would lift his head.
sentences.s562-s19: He had neither possessions nor learning, neither counsel nor skill, nor faith nor the approval of the just.
sentences.s562-s20: May day and night be auspicious for you; may the malicious one's soul be scattered.
sentences.s562-s33: They hung a crown on his head; upon that crown they scattered gold and dirhams.
sentences.s562-s34: Forty days passed for the fair‑faced one; a child came like the shining Sun.
sentences.s562-s35: To him his mobed gave the name Shapur; in that joy they held a feast.
sentences.s562-s36: You said, 'This farr is divine.' Go — the shelter of the standard is wise.
sentences.s562-s13: If you choose companions from the lazy, you will not be useful for work, nor a seeker of the world, nor one who counts (weighs) people.
sentences.s562-s14: Consider yourself: you have no greatness; and if you ever attain (it), you will not become something to behold.
sentences.s562-s15: When a man becomes ill-tempered, he despises the poor man; he always sees that as the doing of ill fortune.
sentences.s562-s16: Year after year idle and lamenting because of fate — neither counsel nor learning nor the splendour of the throne.
sentences.s562-s25: Thus it was: as long as the turning wheel (of fate) moved, it was able at every task and we were powerless.
sentences.s562-s26: They mourned him for forty days; at times they treated him with contempt.
sentences.s562-s27: For many a while the throne lay empty; the chiefs' heads were full of care.
sentences.s562-s28: The mobed kept watch in the king's bedchamber; he saw a tulip‑faced beauty, shining like the moon.
sentences.s562-s29: The tip of her eyelash was like a Kabul dagger; her two locks twisted like strokes of Mongol script.
sentences.s562-s30: Woven in continued succession, one into another; a knot was tied upon it, and she held her head aloft.
sentences.s562-s31: The fairy‑faced woman had a child hidden within her; because of that fair face the world rejoiced.
sentences.s562-s32: Forty days had passed, and they wished to set up a royal throne.
sentences.s562-s21: The great elders gave cries of praise; they themselves attended to the kings' mourning.
sentences.s562-s22: When nine years had passed over the sky, the flower turned yellow — that is, its face became like a pomegranate's.
sentences.s562-s23: A grief came from the death of that merchant; he died, and there was no son for the kingship.
sentences.s562-s24: Thus a renowned, sweet-tongued man came to be; he made the old house new.
